 {"id":"wa-br5u","title":"Custom workflows: YAML/TOML definitions with step sequences","description":"# Custom workflows

## Purpose
Allow users to define custom workflows in YAML/TOML without writing Rust code.

## Workflow Definition Format
```yaml
# ~/.config/wa/workflows/my_custom_workflow.yaml
name: my_custom_workflow
description: "Handle our custom agent's restart prompt"
triggers:
  - event_type: "my-org.restart_prompt"
    agent_types: ["custom-agent"]

steps:
  - name: wait_for_stable
    action: wait
    condition:
      type: pane_idle
      timeout: 5s
  
  - name: send_restart_command
    action: send_text
    text: "/restart\n"
    policy: require_approval  # or allow, deny
  
  - name: verify_restart
    action: wait
    condition:
      type: pattern
      pattern: "Agent restarted successfully"
      timeout: 30s

on_failure:
  action: notify
  message: "Custom workflow failed at step: ${failed_step}"
```

## Step Actions
```yaml
# Available actions
actions:
  - send_text      # Send keystrokes to pane
  - wait           # Wait for condition
  - notify         # Send notification
  - log            # Write to audit log
  - abort          # Stop workflow
  - conditional    # If/else branching
  - loop           # Repeat steps
```

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct YamlWorkflow {
    name: String,
    description: String,
    triggers: Vec<WorkflowTrigger>,
    steps: Vec<WorkflowStep>,
    on_failure: Option<FailureHandler>,
}

impl Workflow for YamlWorkflow {
    async fn execute(&self, ctx: &mut WorkflowContext) -> Result<WorkflowResult> {
        for step in &self.steps {
            match self.execute_step(step, ctx).await {
                Ok(StepResult::Continue) => continue,
                Ok(StepResult::Abort) => break,
                Err(e) => {
                    if let Some(handler) = &self.on_failure {
                        handler.execute(ctx, &e).await?;
                    }
                    return Err(e);
                }
            }
        }
        Ok(WorkflowResult::Success)
    }
}
```

## Testing
- Unit tests for YAML parsing
- Step action tests
- Conditional and loop tests
- Failure handling tests

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] YAML format supports all required actions
- [ ] Workflows load from user directory
- [ ] Steps execute in sequence
- [ ] Conditions work correctly
- [ ] Failure handlers execute
- [ ] Documentation covers workflow authoring
- [ ] Tests cover all step types","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:55:02.320177158Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.224149-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-08T09:16:18.977080405Z"}
 {"id":"wa-brc7d","title":"[EPIC] asupersync Migration in FrankenTerm Crates — Replace smol/async-io/tokio","description":"# [EPIC] asupersync Migration in FrankenTerm Crates

## Why
FrankenTerm crates currently use smol (promise, mux, config) and async-io (async_ossl, wezterm-uds) for async. The project-wide strategy is to replace ALL async runtimes with asupersync (wa-e34d9). This epic covers the FrankenTerm-specific crate migrations.

## Context
- smol is used in: promise (core), mux (spawning), config (async config reload), codec (async PDU I/O)
- async-io is used in: async_ossl (OpenSSL async wrapper), wezterm-uds (Unix domain sockets)
- wezterm-ssh uses tokio via libssh2-sys — this is the hardest migration since libssh2 is C
- asupersync provides: Cx (capability context), structured concurrency, backpressure, cancellation

## Migration order
1. promise crate (foundation — other crates depend on it)
2. codec crate (PDU I/O — used by mux)
3. async_ossl + wezterm-uds (I/O primitives)
4. config crate (async config reload)
5. mux crate (spawning, domain management)
6. wezterm-ssh (hardest — C library async integration)

## Depends on
- wa-e34d9: asupersync must be ready (foundation + Cx infrastructure)
- wa-2umk2: FrankenTerm must be in-tree (we're modifying these crates)

## Strategy: feature-flagged dual-runtime
Like the Lua strategy, use feature flags during migration:
- `async-smol` (default initially): existing smol code
- `async-asupersync`: new asupersync code
- Once all crates migrated and tested, flip default and eventually remove smol","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:36.684463Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:41.163766Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d","depends_on_id":"wa-e34d9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:41.062387Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d","depends_on_id":"wa-2umk2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:41.163683Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-brc7d.1","title":"Replace smol with asupersync in promise crate","description":"# Replace smol with asupersync in FrankenTerm promise crate

## Why
The promise crate (frankenterm/promise/) is the async foundation for all FrankenTerm crates. It provides `SpawnFunc`, `Promise<T>`, and async task spawning. Currently uses smol's block_on and spawn. Must be migrated first because mux, config, and codec depend on it.

## Current state
- frankenterm/promise/Cargo.toml: depends on smol
- frankenterm/promise/src/lib.rs: uses smol::block_on, smol::spawn
- frankenterm/promise/src/spawn.rs: SpawnFunc wraps smol executor

## What to change
1. Replace `smol::block_on` with `asupersync::block_on` (or Cx-based equivalent)
2. Replace `smol::spawn` with asupersync structured concurrency
3. Replace `smol::Timer` with asupersync timer
4. Feature flag: `async-smol` (default) vs `async-asupersync`
5. Both paths must produce identical behavior

## Key consideration
The promise crate's API must remain stable — it's used by every other FrankenTerm crate. The migration is internal implementation, not API change.

## Testing
- All existing promise tests pass with both feature flags
- Promise resolution works correctly with asupersync executor
- Cancellation behavior is preserved
- No leaked tasks after scope exit

## Test framework requirements

### LabRuntime DPOR — concurrent promise resolution
Use LabRuntime with DPOR exploration to verify:
- Concurrent resolution of multiple promises never produces lost wakeups
- Promise::poll from multiple threads is linearizable (only one thread sees Ready)
- Cancellation of a promise while another thread resolves it never panics or leaks
- SpawnFunc task completion is visible to all waiters in bounded time

### criterion benchmarks — promise creation/resolution
Benchmark with criterion:
- Promise<T> creation time (target: <100ns per promise)
- Promise resolution (set value + wake) latency (target: <100ns)
- SpawnFunc overhead vs raw executor spawn (target: <2x overhead)
- Throughput: promises created and resolved per second (target: >10M/sec)

### proptest — promise state machine transitions
Use proptest to generate arbitrary sequences of promise operations (create, poll, resolve, cancel, drop) and verify:
- A promise is resolved at most once (no double-resolution)
- Polling a resolved promise always returns Ready with the correct value
- Dropping a promise before resolution does not leak the pending task
- State transitions follow the expected FSM: Pending -> {Resolved, Cancelled}","notes":"Added optional async-asupersync feature + block_on implementation in promise crate (commit 7f269ee).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:53.225259Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T01:56:05.272479-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T01:56:05.272482-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.1","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:53.225259Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
-{"id":"wa-brc7d.2","title":"Replace smol with asupersync in codec crate","description":"# Replace smol with asupersync in FrankenTerm codec crate

## Why
The codec crate (frankenterm/codec/) handles PDU serialization and async I/O for the mux wire protocol. Uses smol for async read/write. Must be migrated before mux.

## Current state
- Uses smol::io::AsyncRead/AsyncWrite traits
- PDU framing with async read/write
- Compression (zstd) in the I/O pipeline

## What to change
1. Replace smol async I/O traits with asupersync equivalents
2. Update async read/write implementations
3. Feature flag: `async-smol` vs `async-asupersync`
4. Ensure PDU framing is identical regardless of runtime

## Testing
- PDU round-trip tests pass with both runtimes
- Compression/decompression works correctly
- Large PDU handling (>64KB) works
- Connection drop handling is correct

## Test framework requirements

### criterion benchmarks — encode/decode throughput
Benchmark with criterion:
- PDU encode throughput for typical message sizes (target: >500MB/s)
- PDU decode throughput for typical message sizes (target: >500MB/s)
- Compression ratio and throughput for zstd pipeline (target: >200MB/s with >2x compression)
- Large PDU handling (64KB, 256KB, 1MB) encode/decode latency
- Async framing overhead vs synchronous encode/decode

### proptest — codec roundtrip invariants
Use proptest to generate arbitrary PDU payloads and verify:
- encode followed by decode produces the original PDU (roundtrip identity: encode->decode = identity)
- Corrupted bytes in the stream produce Err, never panics or garbage data
- Partial reads (incomplete frames) are handled correctly and resume after more data arrives
- All PDU type variants round-trip correctly, including edge cases (empty payload, max-size payload)
- Compression is transparent: compressed and uncompressed PDUs decode identically","notes":"Closed via commit 865a54e (2026-02-11): frankenterm/codec supports mutually-exclusive features async-smol (default) and async-asupersync with compile-time guards; async leb128 reader uses read_exact for asupersync compatibility; workspace chrono enables serde so mux builds under codec checks. Verified: cargo fmt -p codec --check; cargo check, clippy, and test for codec in both feature modes.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:00.685511Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T02:44:35.174894-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T02:44:35.1749-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.2","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:00.685511Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.2","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:48.980803Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}

Verified: cargo fmt -p codec --check; cargo check, clippy, and test for codec in both feature modes.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:00.685511Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T02:48:46.819729-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T02:48:46.819731-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.2","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:00.685511Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.2","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:48.980803Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-brc7d.3","title":"Replace async-io with asupersync in async_ossl and wezterm-uds","description":"# Replace async-io in async_ossl and wezterm-uds

## Why
These two crates provide async I/O primitives used by the mux and SSH systems:
- async_ossl: Async OpenSSL wrapper (TLS for mux connections)
- wezterm-uds: Async Unix domain socket handling

## Current state
- async_ossl/src/lib.rs: Uses async-io for polling OpenSSL BIO
- wezterm-uds/src/lib.rs: Uses async-io for Unix socket polling

## What to change
1. Replace async-io polling with asupersync I/O polling (Cx-based)
2. OpenSSL BIO integration: map SSL_read/SSL_write to asupersync futures
3. Unix socket: map connect/accept/read/write to asupersync
4. Feature flag both crates: `async-io` vs `async-asupersync`

## Key consideration
These crates deal with raw file descriptors and system calls. The asupersync I/O reactor must support registering arbitrary FDs for read/write readiness, similar to what async-io provides via Async<T>.

## macOS platform note
On macOS, the I/O notification backend must use **kqueue** instead of Linux's epoll. The asupersync I/O reactor should abstract over this via a platform-specific poller:
- Linux: epoll_create1 / epoll_ctl / epoll_wait
- macOS/BSD: kqueue / kevent
- Both async_ossl and wezterm-uds must be tested on macOS to ensure kqueue-based FD registration works correctly for OpenSSL BIOs and Unix domain sockets
- Note: macOS Unix domain socket paths are limited to 104 bytes (vs 108 on Linux)

## Testing
- TLS handshake works with asupersync
- Unix socket connection and data transfer works
- Verify no FD leaks after connection close
- Test with real mux server connection

## Test framework requirements

### criterion benchmarks — I/O throughput
Benchmark with criterion:
- TLS handshake latency with asupersync vs async-io baseline (target: <5ms)
- Unix domain socket throughput (target: >1GB/s for local IPC)
- OpenSSL BIO read/write throughput through async wrapper (target: >500MB/s)
- FD registration/deregistration overhead in the I/O reactor

### LabRuntime DPOR — concurrent I/O operations
Use LabRuntime with DPOR exploration to verify:
- Concurrent TLS connections never deadlock on BIO polling
- Concurrent Unix socket accept + read/write is linearizable
- FD close during pending read/write produces clean errors, no use-after-close
- Multiple tasks sharing an I/O reactor do not starve each other","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:10.599664Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:39:14.257695Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.3","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:10.599664Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-brc7d.4","title":"Replace smol with asupersync in config crate","description":"# Replace smol with asupersync in FrankenTerm config crate

## Why
The config crate uses smol for async config file watching and reload. 

## What to change
1. Replace smol file watcher with asupersync equivalent
2. Replace smol timer for debounced reload
3. Feature flag: `async-smol` vs `async-asupersync`
4. Config reload callback mechanism must work with both runtimes

## Testing
- Config file modification triggers reload with both runtimes
- Debounce works correctly (rapid changes → single reload)
- Config errors don't crash the watcher

## Test framework requirements

### proptest — config parsing roundtrips
Use proptest to generate arbitrary config structures (with valid and edge-case field values) and verify:
- Serialize to TOML then parse back produces the original config (roundtrip identity)
- Invalid field values produce descriptive errors, never panics
- Unknown keys are ignored (forward compatibility for config evolution)
- Default values are correctly applied for missing fields
- Nested config sections (colors, fonts, keybindings) all round-trip correctly

### criterion benchmarks — config hot-reload latency
Benchmark with criterion:
- Config file parse time for typical frankenterm.toml (target: <1ms)
- Hot-reload end-to-end latency: file change detection to config applied (target: <50ms)
- Debounce timer accuracy: verify rapid changes coalesce correctly
- Config diff computation: detect which fields changed (target: <100us)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:17.393741Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:39:21.996251Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.4","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:17.393741Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.4","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:49.175457Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-brc7d.5","title":"Replace smol with asupersync in mux crate","description":"# Replace smol with asupersync in FrankenTerm mux crate

## Why
The mux crate is the largest consumer of smol. It uses async for: domain management, pane I/O, tab management, mux server communication, and task spawning. This is the most complex migration.

## Current state
- Uses smol::spawn for background tasks
- Uses smol channels for pane I/O
- Uses smol::Timer for timeouts
- Uses promise crate (which wraps smol)
- Domain trait has async methods

## What to change
1. Replace all smol::spawn with asupersync structured concurrency
2. Replace smol channels with asupersync channels
3. Replace smol::Timer with asupersync time (via Cx)
4. Update Domain trait async methods for asupersync
5. Feature flag: `async-smol` vs `async-asupersync`

## Dependencies
- promise crate must be migrated first (wa-brc7d.1)
- codec crate must be migrated first (wa-brc7d.2)
- async_ossl must be migrated for TLS domains (wa-brc7d.3)

## Testing
- All mux tests pass with both runtimes
- Domain connection/disconnection works
- Pane I/O (read/write) works
- Tab creation/destruction works
- Concurrent multi-pane operation works
- No task leaks after tab close

## Test framework requirements

### LabRuntime DPOR — mux concurrency
Use LabRuntime with DPOR exploration to verify:
- Concurrent pane read/write across multiple tabs never deadlocks
- Domain disconnect during active pane I/O produces clean errors, no orphaned tasks
- Tab close while pane I/O is in-flight cancels all pending operations
- Channel send/receive ordering is preserved under concurrent multi-producer scenarios
- Task spawning and structured concurrency scopes do not leak tasks on cancellation

### criterion benchmarks — mux operation throughput
Benchmark with criterion:
- Pane I/O throughput: bytes read/written per second through mux layer (target: >500MB/s local)
- Tab creation/destruction latency (target: <500us per tab)
- Domain connection establishment latency (target: <10ms local, <100ms remote)
- Channel throughput for pane I/O pipeline (target: >1M messages/sec)

### Loom model checking — shared state
Use Loom to exhaustively verify the mux crate's shared state synchronization:
- Arc<Mutex<Tab>> access patterns are deadlock-free
- Domain registry (shared HashMap) concurrent access is safe
- Pane ID generation (AtomicU64) is monotonic and unique under contention
- No data races in the pane I/O buffer management

## Cross-references
- **wa-2dd4s.5** — FrankenMux integration: the mux crate provides the async runtime foundation that FrankenMux's unified tab model builds upon; this migration must complete before FrankenMux integration can use asupersync
- **wa-brc7d.1** — promise crate migration: must complete first as mux depends on promise
- **wa-brc7d.2** — codec crate migration: must complete first as mux depends on codec for wire protocol
- **wa-brc7d.3** — async_ossl/wezterm-uds migration: must complete first for TLS domain connections","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:27.387986Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:39:38.117734Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.5","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:27.387986Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.5","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:49.297251Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.5","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:49.402757Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-brc7d.5","depends_on_id":"wa-brc7d.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:40:49.515142Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
